scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced the shoot day through readiness, shooting, wrap, and completion only when permits, call-sheet readiness, and report-package evidence were explicit.
Missing permit-release, insurance, or neighborhood-notice prerequisites kept the day blocked and produced a bounded exception summary instead of an unsupported go-ahead.
A call-sheet and stripboard mismatch was treated as a production-control exception and prevented unsupported progression on a stale revision.
Completion was refused until DPR, departmental reports, timekeeping, and performer paperwork existed, keeping the lifecycle state reversible when evidence failed.
Creative and specialty approval requests were rejected cleanly and routed without widening the lane into directing, locations, or stunt authority.
Safety-sensitive activity was blocked when coordinator, bulletin, or permit evidence was missing and the lane escalated instead of optimizing for schedule.
Missing SAG-AFTRA signatory or cast-clearance evidence blocked performer work and preserved the public-baseline paperwork and clearance requirements.
Meal, turnaround, and payroll pressure was packaged as a labor-boundary escalation rather than being treated as routine schedule recovery.
The lane refused to absorb post-production or release work simply because the industry is film and streaming, proving that the industry name is not cosmetic.
Conflicting permit terms and tenant policy were summarized and escalated instead of being merged into a false approval answer.
Tenant terminology was adapted without losing DGA, SAG-AFTRA, film-office, or safety baseline controls and evidence requirements.
A crew-compliance evidence gap was handled as a readiness blocker tied to Contract Services or the tenant-approved equivalent rather than as generic staffing.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to call sheets, permits, signatory clearance, training proof, daily reports, and wrap evidence.
Functionality coverage includes all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and the required cross-industry ability for artifact-aware, boundary-safe execution.
